feed.title: Hilda Sandström
feed.subtitle: Computational chemist at TU Munich. Marie Curie MSCA fellow working on data-driven identification of atmospheric compounds with quantum chemistry and machine learning.

entry.title: Concluded spring trainings
entry.content:
Before summer vacation, a quick recap of the training I’ve done this year.

I finished two German courses at the TUM Language Center, going from A1.2 in autumn 2025 to A2.2 this spring. I was really looking forward to the Doctoral Supervision Training, a joint leadership course run by Helmholtz, the Max Planck Society, LMU Munich and TUM, and it delivered: coaching styles, communication and feedback techniques, and health, all things you run into constantly when working in teams or supervising students. I also did a TUM ProLehre workshop on supervising student theses.

The most immersive one was the CSC Summer School in High Performance Computing, ten days in Nuuksio, Finland learning HPC programming with C++. There was also sauna, hiking, canoeing, lake swimming and karaoke, so a great way to end the season before summer break.
entry.summary: HPC summer school, doctoral supervision training, and two German courses before the summer break.

entry.title: Contributed talk at JpGU-AGU Joint Meeting 2026 in Chiba

link.title: Contributed talk at JpGU-AGU Joint Meeting 2026 in Chiba

entry.content:
Spent a week in Chiba, Japan for the JpGU-AGU Joint Meeting 2026. It was a really well organized event, and great to see the breadth of atmospheric chemistry research happening in Japan. I gave a contributed talk, Towards atmospheric compound identification using simulated electron ionization mass spectra, and also served as a poster judge for the meeting.

After the conference, I joined a two day excursion to the volcanoes Hakone and Fuji, organized as part of the meeting and led by Prof. Kazutaka Mannen of the Hot Springs Research Institute of Kanagawa Prefecture.
entry.summary: Spent a week in Chiba, Japan for the JpGU-AGU Joint Meeting 2026. It was a really well organized event, and great to see the breadth of atmospheric chemistry research happening in Japan. I gave a contributed talk, Towards atmospheric compound identification using simulated electron ionization mass spectra, and also served as a poster judge for the meeting.

entry.title: Invited talk at University of Gothenburg

link.title: Invited talk at University of Gothenburg

entry.content: Gave an invited departmental seminar at the University of Gothenburg, where I spent a year as a visiting researcher in Mattias Hallquist’s group before starting at TUM. The talk, Data-driven mass spectrometry for atmospheric compound identification, was a nice chance to catch up with everyone and share where the mass spectrometry work has gone since.
entry.summary: Gave an invited departmental seminar at the University of Gothenburg, where I spent a year as a visiting researcher in Mattias Hallquist’s group before starting at TUM. The talk, Data-driven mass spectrometry for atmospheric compound identification, was a nice chance to catch up with everyone and share where the mass spectrometry work has gone since.

entry.title: Invited talk at University of Glasgow

link.title: Invited talk at University of Glasgow

entry.content: Gave an invited talk at the University of Glasgow as part of the Network on Mathematical Data Science for Materials Science workshop (Workshop on the Interface of Mathematics and Machine Learning for Applications in Materials Science). The talk, Data-driven compound identification with atmospheric mass spectrometry, covered our recent work on machine learning approaches for identifying atmospheric compounds using mass spectrometry data.
entry.summary: Gave an invited talk at the University of Glasgow as part of the Network on Mathematical Data Science for Materials Science workshop (Workshop on the Interface of Mathematics and Machine Learning for Applications in Materials Science). The talk, Data-driven compound identification with atmospheric mass spectrometry, covered our recent work on machine learning approaches for identifying atmospheric compounds using mass spectrometry data.

entry.title: Contributed talk at Chemical Compounds Space Conference 2026

link.title: Contributed talk at Chemical Compounds Space Conference 2026

entry.content: Gave a contributed talk at the Chemical Compounds Space Conference (CCSC 2026) in Munich, Germany. The talk, Towards atmospheric compound identification using simulated electron ionization mass spectra, presented our work on using simulated EI mass spectra for improved identification of atmospheric compounds.
entry.summary: Gave a contributed talk at the Chemical Compounds Space Conference (CCSC 2026) in Munich, Germany. The talk, Towards atmospheric compound identification using simulated electron ionization mass spectra, presented our work on using simulated EI mass spectra for improved identification of atmospheric compounds.

entry.title: First time thesis opponent in Barcelona and Linus’ first paper out

link.title: First time thesis opponent in Barcelona and Linus’ first paper out

entry.content:
Today I just returned from Barcelona, Spain, where I had the pleasure of serving as thesis opponent for Niccolò Bancone at Universitat Autònoma de Barcelona. His thesis investigated HCN reactivity at forsterite surfaces using computational chemistry. The topic is very close to my own PhD research, so it was a great opportunity to take on the role of thesis opponent for the first time.

Niccolò delivered an great presentation, and I genuinly had fun with the scientific discussion that followed. One clear lesson for next time is that while the actual defence day is a delight, the preparation should be done in a more structured and organized way by me next time, mainly to keep my own stress levels under control in the days prior.

In other good news, Linus, who started his PhD with us in January after completing his undergraduate theses and working in the group as a research assistant, has now published his first first-author paper. The work focuses on developing descriptors suited for property prediction of atmospheric compounds. We show that by using this new and more appropriate descriptor, prediction accuracy improves across a range of molecular properties.

Read the paper here
entry.summary: Today I just returned from Barcelona, Spain, where I had the pleasure of serving as thesis opponent for Niccolò Bancone at Universitat Autònoma de Barcelona. His thesis investigated HCN reactivity at forsterite surfaces using computational chemistry. The topic is very close to my own PhD research, so it was a great opportunity to take on the role of thesis opponent for the first time.

entry.title: Congratulations to Linus Lind
entry.content:
This past month marked the graduation of master student Linus Lind, who joined the group at Aalto in 2023 as a bachelor student and continued as a research assistant to complete his master thesis.

During his time with the group, Linus worked on developing a new molecular descriptor for atmospheric compounds. Just weeks after his graduation, his work has been published as a preprint: arXiv:2510.20465.

It has been so much fun as a supervisor to follow Linus through his studies and theses, and we hope to collaborate more in the future!
entry.summary: This past month marked the graduation of master student Linus Lind, who joined the group at Aalto in 2023 as a bachelor student and continued as a research assistant to complete his master thesis.

entry.title: Guten Tag Munich and TUM
entry.content: This month marks the start of a 2-year position for me at TUM! Here I will conduct my MSCA funded project “Cloudmap”. More updates will come but for now you can read more about the project at CORDIS and enjoy an autumnal picture of my dog Asta, who will accompany me on this new adventure!
entry.summary: Starting my 2-year MSCA-funded project 'Cloudmap' at TUM! Asta joins the adventure.

entry.title: Visit to Gothenburg University
entry.content:
This week marked the end of a visit to Mattias Hallqvist’s group at Gothenburg University. I had the honor to present an invited talk on my research both from Aalto as well as my plans for the upcoming MSCA position.

It is a great group to be around, and the atmosphere was excellent—pun intended!
entry.summary: This week marked the end of a visit to Mattias Hallqvist’s group at Gothenburg University. I had the honor to present an invited talk on my research both from Aalto as well as my plans for the upcoming MSCA position.

entry.title: Paper published in GMD: Similarity-based analysis of atmospheric organic compounds for machine learning applications

link.title: Paper published in GMD: Similarity-based analysis of atmospheric organic compounds for machine learning applications

entry.content:
Our work on mapping out the potential of data-driven compound identification with mass spectrometry in atmospheric chemistry has been published in GMD!

The idea for this project began when we set out to improve compound identification in atmospheric chemistry and realized that other chemistry domains had already been on this track for a decade. Great news—if their models could be applied to our data. But there were open questions: the existing models were trained to map mass spectra to molecular fingerprints (binary representations of molecular structures). Their performance depends heavily on the training data, and as with most machine learning models, assuming they generalize to new compound classes is far from guaranteed.

To address this, I conducted a similarity analysis to qualitatively and quantitatively assess how close the compounds relevant to particle formation are to those used in existing models. The results showed clear distinctions: overall similarity between datasets was low. This was measured using molecular fingerprints and a metric called Tanimoto similarity, and further explored with unsupervised clustering methods. We concluded that these models need careful validation before being applied to atmospheric compounds. The major challenge remains the lack of large, high-quality datasets of atmospheric mass spectra.

The process of bringing this project from idea to publication has been a long but rewarding journey. Along the way, it inspired a perspective paper and a number of grant applications, and provided me with a deep introduction to cheminformatics—something I hadn’t encountered during my PhD. Importantly, it also gave our group at Aalto a solid foundation to take the next steps toward improved compound identification models. Fun to now take the next steps!
entry.summary: Our work on mapping out the potential of data-driven compound identification with mass spectrometry in atmospheric chemistry has been published in GMD!
